summary: I was born in 1917 in a small town in racially segregated Mississippi. Growing up there was difficult because, despite the U.S. Supreme Court ruling against residential segregation ordinances, Jim Crowism was still very strong. Life was marked by segregation and discrimination, which shaped my early experiences before my family moved north in 1922.

During my childhood in Mississippi, the United States Supreme Court had ruled that ordinances enforcing residential segregation violated the Fourteenth Amendment. Despite this ruling, segregation persisted in practice. This legal backdrop contrasted sharply with the reality of Jim Crowism that I experienced growing up.

entity: Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution
summary: The Fourteenth Amendment played a significant role during my early years because the Supreme Court used it to rule against residential segregation ordinances. However, even with this constitutional protection, segregation and discrimination continued in Mississippi and beyond. This tension between law and practice was a defining feature of my youth.

Although the Supreme Court struck down some segregation ordinances, Jim Crow laws and more so Jim Crowism were rampant during my childhood in Mississippi. These laws enforced racial segregation and discrimination, deeply affecting my community and family. The oppressive environment pushed my mother to move us north in search of better opportunities.

After leaving Mississippi in 1922, my family first moved to New York City as part of the Great Migration. There, like in other northern cities, we faced poverty, racism, and de facto segregation. Despite these challenges, New York introduced me to new political and economic realities that shaped my understanding of race and opportunity.

Philadelphia was another stop for my family during our migration north after leaving Mississippi. Similar to New York and Detroit, we encountered poverty and racial discrimination there. These experiences in Philadelphia contributed to my awareness of systemic racism beyond the South.

Detroit was one of the cities my family lived in during our northern migration. Like New York and Philadelphia, Detroit exposed me to the harsh realities of poverty and racial segregation. It was part of the journey that eventually led us to a more stable life in Hamtramck, Michigan.

After moving through several northern cities, my family finally settled in Hamtramck, Michigan, where we found a relatively stable life. This stability allowed me to focus on school and athletics, which eventually earned me a scholarship to the University of Detroit. Hamtramck was an important place in my youth and development.

I attended the University of Detroit on an athletic scholarship and was the only black player on the football team. Unfortunately, in my junior year, I lost the scholarship due to reasons related to race, forcing me to work full-time in an automobile factory to continue my studies. Despite this setback, I persevered and completed my education there.

After my time at the University of Detroit, I earned a degree from Meharry Medical College's School of Dentistry in 1944. This achievement marked a significant milestone in my life, allowing me to pursue a professional career despite earlier hardships. Meharry was instrumental in shaping my future as a dentist and community leader.

I served in the United States Army during World War II and was later recalled to service at the end of the Korean War. My military service was a defining chapter in my life, providing discipline and a sense of duty. After discharge, I and my wife moved to Las Vegas, where I continued my fight against racial discrimination.

During World War II, I served in the United States Army, which was a significant period in my life. This experience came after years of struggling with racial discrimination and helped shape my resolve to fight for civil rights. My service ended before I pursued my activism in Las Vegas.

At the end of the Korean War, I was recalled to serve again in the U.S. Army. This second period of military service reinforced my commitment to justice and equality. After my discharge, my wife and I relocated to Las Vegas, where I became deeply involved in civil rights activism.

My wife and I moved to Las Vegas after my military service, and I was immediately drawn to the city's vibrant, twenty-four-hour lifestyle. However, I quickly encountered the same racial discrimination I had faced all my life. Refusing to accept it, I became a vocal opponent of segregation and was elected president of the local NAACP branch within a year.

entity: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
summary: In Las Vegas, I was elected president of the local branch of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People shortly after arriving. Under my leadership, we took direct and confrontational action to end overt segregation, achieving significant victories by 1960. Although I stepped down in 1961, I continued to advocate for the black community for over four decades.

In 1992, I was elected to the non-partisan Clark County School Board, part of the Clark County School District. Despite being outspoken, I dedicated four years to understanding and managing the complex paperwork and responsibilities of the school board. This role allowed me to continue serving my community in a political capacity.

I worry that the progress made in civil rights since the 1950s could be undone by a conservative backlash similar to what happened during the first Reconstruction era. That period was marked by initial gains for black Americans that were ultimately reversed. This historical lesson motivates me to remain vigilant and active in protecting civil rights.

I have been an unwavering supporter of affirmative action throughout my life, believing it essential to preserving the gains made in civil rights. Affirmative action helps ensure opportunities for minorities in education and employment. My advocacy for it reflects my commitment to equality and justice for the black community.
